metadata.citation: Private Capital Research Institute, Early IPO Data (privatecapitalresearchinstitute.org/pages/research/earlyipo.html); comparable to Ritter (1991) long-run IPO underperformance findings

dlom_support.finding: Post-IPO companies — which are MORE liquid than pre-IPO private companies — still underperform benchmarks by 16.7% (median) within 2 years. This establishes a floor for illiquidity discounts on private companies.

implication_by_stage.pre_seed_seed: DLOM of 30-35% supported: smallest IPOs (Q1) show -14.3% median 3yr abnormal return even WITH public market liquidity. Pre-revenue private companies warrant additional discount.
implication_by_stage.series_a_b: DLOM of 12-20% supported: growth-stage companies (low BTM) show -18.9% median underperformance. Private companies at this stage face both illiquidity and growth-company risk.
implication_by_stage.series_c_plus: DLOM of 7% supported: larger IPOs (Q2+) converge toward benchmark returns. Late-stage private companies approaching IPO face minimal additional illiquidity discount.
implication_by_stage.late_pre_ipo: DLOM of 4% supported: near-IPO companies have a visible liquidity event horizon. Historical data shows abnormal returns narrow as companies mature (year 5-6 median: -5.2% to -5.9%).
